translation: O Prophet, why do you prohibit [yourself from] what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking the approval of your wives? And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ مَآ أَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ لَكَ ۖ تَبْتَغِى مَرْضَاتَ أَزْوَٰجِكَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

This surah begins with a profound address: "O Prophet, why do you forbid what Allah ﷻ has made lawful for you, seeking to please your wives?" Like Surah At-Talaq before it, which addresses the breakdown of love between husband and wife, this surah examines the opposite scenario – when excessive love between spouses begins to compromise religious principles and justice.

The opening is remarkable because instead of transitioning to guidance for the believers, Allah ﷻ addresses something the Prophet ﷺ himself was doing. This relates to a famous incident involving two of the Mothers of the Believers. The historical context involves two separate but related situations that come together in these ayahs.

The first incident concerns the Prophet's ﷺ equitable distribution of time among his wives. One of the Mothers of the Believers had prepared a special honey drink for him, and he spent some extra time at her residence enjoying it. When he visited another wife's home, she detected the scent and expressed disapproval, saying it smelled unpleasant. Out of love and consideration for his wife's feelings, the Prophet ﷺ decided he would never consume that honey again. This wasn't a religious prohibition in the legal sense – it was simply a personal preference made out of courtesy and respect for his spouse.

However, the Prophet's ﷺ behavior exists under constant scrutiny. Muslims observe everything he does and derive religious rulings from his actions. There's historical precedent for this kind of misunderstanding. The Companions (RA) were so devoted to following the Prophetic example that they would avoid eating certain fruits simply because they never witnessed the Prophet ﷺ cutting them – not knowing whether there was a proper Sunnah method for doing so. One Companion (RA) would lean to one side whenever he passed a certain road in Madinah because a tree once stood there, and the Prophet ﷺ used to bend that way when passing by it.

This wasn't unique to the Muslim community. Allah ﷻ mentions in the Quran that all food was lawful for the Children of Israel except what Israel (Prophet Yaqub AS) made forbidden upon himself. Prophet Yaqub (AS) simply didn't like the taste of camel meat, so he avoided it personally. His descendants then transformed this personal preference into a religious prohibition, making camel meat haram in their understanding of the law.

For prophets, even the smallest habits carry immense weight because they establish religious precedents. If the Prophet ﷺ declares he won't eat honey anymore as a personal gesture to his wife, this could be misunderstood as a religious ruling, leading the entire community to avoid honey. This is why prophets must be extraordinarily cautious about even minor personal choices – they could leave lasting religious legacies unintentionally.

When Allah ﷻ says, "Why do you forbid what Allah has made lawful for you?" He is addressing this deeper issue. The closing of the ayah – "And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" – contains within it both correction and profound compliment. Everyone should be concerned with ensuring the Messenger ﷺ is pleased with them, yet here is the Messenger worried about whether his wife is upset. He's willing to give up something he enjoys simply to maintain harmony. Even in this moment of Divine correction, Allah ﷻ acknowledges the beauty of his character and his deep love for his family.

The ayah teaches us a crucial principle: don't forbid upon yourself what Allah ﷻ has made permissible simply to save a relationship or to please a spouse. Such unnatural conditions won't last. You won't be able to maintain them, and they create unnecessary complications in your life and potentially in the religion itself.

translation: Allah has already ordained for you [Muslims] the dissolution of your oaths. And Allah is your protector, and He is the Knowing, the Wise
commentary:
قَدْ فَرَضَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ تَحِلَّةَ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

"Allah has already mandated for you the releasing of your oaths. And Allah is your Protector, and He is the Knowing, the Wise."

This ayah provides the solution to the situation described in the first ayah. When the Prophet ﷺ essentially took an oath by saying he would never consume that honey again, Allah ﷻ instructs that this oath must be released. Normally, when someone breaks an oath, there's an expiation (kaffarah) that must be paid. However, Allah ﷻ mandates that this particular oath be opened without dwelling on the usual consequences.

The phrase "Allah has already mandated for you" uses the plural form, meaning that while this revelation addresses the Prophet's ﷺ specific situation, the teaching extends to all believers. Don't place unnatural conditions on yourself for the sake of preserving a relationship. Such restrictions, when they go against what Allah ﷻ has made permissible and easy, become unsustainable.

Sometimes out of a desire to please our spouse, we agree to conditions we shouldn't normally accept. We might promise to give up certain permissible things or take on obligations that are beyond our natural capacity. Allah ﷻ is teaching us not to put ourselves in these unnatural situations. If you've already done so, the pathway to releasing such oaths exists within the Shariah.

The ayah concludes by reminding us that "Allah is your Protector" – He is your ultimate guardian and the One looking out for your best interests. "He is the Knowing, the Wise" – He knows what's best for you and His commands contain perfect wisdom. This isn't about being harsh or unsympathetic to marital harmony. Rather, it's about establishing healthy, sustainable boundaries that protect both the relationship and one's religious obligations.

translation: And [remember] when the Prophet confided to one of his wives a statement; and when she informed [another] of it and Allah showed it to him, he made known part of it and ignored a part. And when he informed her about it, she said, "Who told you this?" He said, "I was informed by the Knowing, the Acquainted
commentary:
وَإِذْ أَسَرَّ ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِلَىٰ بَعْضِ أَزْوَٰجِهِۦ حَدِيثًا فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَتْ بِهِۦ وَأَظْهَرَهُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ عَرَّفَ بَعْضَهُۥ وَأَعْرَضَ عَنۢ بَعْضٍ ۖ فَلَمَّا نَبَّأَهَا بِهِۦ قَالَتْ مَنْ أَنۢبَأَكَ هَـٰذَا ۖ قَالَ نَبَّأَنِىَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ

"And when the Prophet confided to one of his wives a statement, and when she informed [another] of it and Allah showed it to him, he made known part of it and ignored a part. And when he informed her about it, she said, 'Who told you this?' He said, 'I was informed by the Knowing, the Aware.'"

This ayah addresses the second incident, which involves the sharing of a secret. The Prophet ﷺ confided something private to one of his wives – this involved Hafsah and Aishah (RA), the Mothers of the Believers. When the secret was shared with the other, Allah ﷻ informed His Messenger ﷺ that his confidence had been breached.

The sharing of secrets happens sometimes in very innocent ways. Someone tells you something confidential and adds, "Don't tell anyone." But then in the flow of conversation with a close friend or family member, it slips out. You might preface it with "This is top secret" before sharing it with someone else, not fully grasping that you've now violated the trust placed in you. This kind of slip is very human and very common, especially among those who are close to one another.

What makes this ayah profound is how it describes the Prophet's ﷺ response. When Allah ﷻ revealed to him what had happened – that his secret had been shared – he didn't confront his wife with full, explicit details. The ayah says: "He made known part of it and ignored a part." He dropped a hint that he knew what had transpired, but he didn't lay out every specific detail. He didn't say, "Allah revealed to me that you spoke to so-and-so and said exactly these words." He communicated indirectly, giving her the opportunity to understand and reflect.

This is remarkable wisdom in handling marital conflicts. The Prophet ﷺ could have come with the full weight of Divine revelation and detailed exactly what happened, but instead he chose a gentler approach. He made it clear enough that she would understand he knew, but subtle enough to preserve dignity and allow for reconciliation.

Her response reveals something deeply human about how we react when caught doing something wrong. When he finally made it clear that he knew about the breach of confidence, she said, "Who told you this?" The natural human reaction isn't always immediate repentance. Often, our first instinct is to deflect – to find out who revealed our mistake. It's the transfer of guilt: "I told her not to tell anyone! Who squealed on me?"

This happens commonly, even with children. When you discover they've done something they weren't supposed to do, their first question is often "Who told you?" rather than "I'm sorry." The sorry comes afterwards, if at all. First comes the need to know who exposed the secret.

The Prophet's ﷺ response is perfect: "The Knowing, the Aware informed me." He attributes the knowledge to Allah ﷻ using two of His Divine names that emphasize His complete knowledge. Al-'Aleem (the All-Knowing) and Al-Khabeer (the All-Aware) – the One who knows everything, including what's hidden, and Who is thoroughly informed of all matters.

This response serves multiple purposes. First, it reminds his wife that this isn't just about a marital dispute or someone tattling. Allah ﷻ Himself is involved, and He sees and knows all things. Second, it elevates the conversation from a mere personal conflict to a matter of accountability before Allah ﷻ. Third, it's a gentle but firm reminder that even though they are husband and wife, he is also the Messenger of Allah ﷻ, and she is a Mother of the Believers with a unique responsibility.

This interaction teaches us something essential about marriage in Islam, especially for those striving to live according to Islamic values. Yes, these are intimate, personal conversations between husband and wife. Yes, they involve very human emotions, feelings and mistakes. But even the Mothers of the Believers had to remember that their husband wasn't just their spouse – he was the Messenger of Allah ﷻ. Similarly, he had to remember that while addressing his wives' concerns, he was also setting an example for the entire Ummah in how to handle marital conflicts with wisdom, mercy and justice.

translation: If you two [wives] repent to Allah, [it is best], for your hearts have deviated. But if you cooperate against him - then indeed Allah is his protector, and Gabriel and the righteous of the believers and the angels, moreover, are [his] assistants
commentary:
إِن تَتُوبَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُمَا ۖ وَإِن تَظَـٰهَرَا عَلَيْهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلَىٰهُ وَجِبْرِيلُ وَصَـٰلِحُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ

Now Allah ﷻ speaks directly to both mothers of the believers. Notice the remarkable shift - up until this point, the conversation was between the Prophet ﷺ and his wives. But because this matter could affect not just the household but the entire mission of Islam, Allah ﷻ Himself takes over this conversation.

The ayah begins: "إِن تَتُوبَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ" - "If both of you make tawbah to Allah." The word tawbah comes from the Arabic root meaning to lean or incline toward something. Allah ﷻ is saying that if they were to turn back to Him in repentance, "فَقَدْ صَغَتْ قُلُوبُكُمَا" - "your hearts have already inclined in that direction."

This is a remarkable insight into human psychology and divine mercy. Allah ﷻ acknowledges that sometimes people do something wrong and feel remorse inside, but they need encouragement and courage to openly admit it and seek forgiveness. Even someone who has committed a wrong can feel bad about it internally. How much more so for the mothers of the believers, who were among the most righteous women to ever live? Their hearts already felt the weight of what had happened. They just needed to bring it out and make proper tawbah.

Then comes a stern warning: "وَإِن تَظَـٰهَرَا عَلَيْهِ" - "And if you both gang up against him." The word تَظَـٰهَرَا suggests cooperation, banding together. Aisha and Hafsah (RA) were very close to each other outside of this incident. But if they were to unite against the Prophet ﷺ, if they were to pressure him or make his life difficult at home, then Allah ﷻ makes it clear where His support lies.

"فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلَىٰهُ" - "Indeed Allah is his protecting friend." Then the support escalates: "وَجِبْرِيلُ" - "and Jibreel (AS)," "وَصَـٰلِحُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ" - "and the righteous among all believers," "وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ ظَهِيرٌ" - "and the angels after that are his supporters."

What's remarkable about this ayah is that it doesn't say "قُل لَهُم" - "Say to them." Allah ﷻ addresses the mothers of the believers directly. This is because the Prophet ﷺ is not just a husband. If stress at home were to affect him, it might impact his teaching, his preaching, his thinking, his mission outside the home. And that cannot be allowed to happen. The entire armies of Allah ﷻ would be mobilized to prevent this rebellion at home from affecting the Prophet's ﷺ work. This demonstrates the immense status of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

translation: Perhaps his Lord, if he divorced you [all], would substitute for him wives better than you - submitting [to Allah], believing, devoutly obedient, repentant, worshipping, and traveling - [ones] previously married and virgins
commentary:
عَسَىٰ رَبُّهُۥٓ إِن طَلَّقَكُنَّ أَن يُبْدِلَهُۥٓ أَزْوَٰجًا خَيْرًا مِّنكُنَّ مُسْلِمَـٰتٍ مُّؤْمِنَـٰتٍ قَـٰنِتَـٰتٍ تَـٰٓئِبَـٰتٍ عَـٰبِدَٰتٍ سَـٰٓئِحَـٰتٍ ثَيِّبَـٰتٍ وَأَبْكَارًا

Then comes another warning, one meant to shake the hearts of the mothers of the believers: "عَسَىٰ رَبُّهُۥٓ إِن طَلَّقَكُنَّ" - "It may be that his Master, if he were to divorce you..." 

Notice the word "عَسَىٰ" - "it may be" or "perhaps." This is different from "إِذَا", which indicates something that will definitely happen. The word asa suggests possibility, not certainty. The Prophet ﷺ wouldn't do this, but the possibility is raised to create awareness and concern.

"أَن يُبْدِلَهُۥٓ أَزْوَٰجًا خَيْرًا مِّنكُنَّ" - "That He will replace for him spouses better than you." Allah ﷻ might give him better wives than you. Then comes a stunning list of qualities, presented in a unique grammatical structure.

The qualities are listed: "مُسْلِمَـٰتٍ" - Muslim women who have submitted entirely to Allah, "مُّؤْمِنَـٰتٍ" - women of true faith whose iman remains constant, "قَـٰنِتَـٰتٍ" - women who are subservient to Allah ﷻ and to those He has given authority over them, including their husband (in this case, the Messenger ﷺ who deserves complete respect not just as a husband but as the Prophet of Allah), "تَـٰٓئِبَـٰتٍ" - women who, when they make mistakes, turn back to Allah in tawbah, which brings them even closer to Him, "عَـٰبِدَٰتٍ" - women who engage in worship, and as their worship increases, they develop more love for Allah ﷻ and less attachment to worldly things, "سَـٰٓئِحَـٰتٍ" - women who become spiritual and otherworldly (the word saihaat literally means those who travel or fast, referring to leaving worldly pleasures and appetites behind), and finally "ثَيِّبَـٰتٍ وَأَبْكَارًا" - previously married and previously unmarried.

What's remarkable about this grammatical structure is that there's no "wa" (and) between most of these qualities until the very end. In Arabic grammar, when you have a list without the conjunction, it means all these qualities exist simultaneously. But you cannot be both previously married and previously unmarried at the same time, so those two are separated with "wa" at the end.

Here's the profound lesson: this warning was given, but the Prophet ﷺ never replaced his wives. What does that tell us? That the mothers of the believers possessed these very qualities. If they didn't have these qualities, they would have been worthy of replacement. But they weren't replaced, which means they were indeed muslimaat, mu'minaat, qaanitaat, taa'ibaat, 'aabidaat, saa'ihaat - Aisha (RA) was previously unmarried, and others like Khadijah (RA) were previously married.

But this ayah isn't just about the Prophet's ﷺ household. These attributes describe what every Muslim woman should aspire toward. These are the qualities of a truly believing woman.

translation: O you who have believed, protect yourselves and your families from a Fire whose fuel is people and stones, over which are [appointed] angels, harsh and severe; they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them but do what they are commanded
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ قُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ نَارًا وَقُودُهَا ٱلنَّاسُ وَٱلْحِجَارَةُ عَلَيْهَا مَلَـٰٓئِكَةٌ غِلَاظٌ شِدَادٌ لَّا يَعْصُونَ ٱللَّهَ مَآ أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ

Now the address shifts dramatically. What seemed like a personal matter in the Prophet's ﷺ household becomes a universal command for all believers. This is one of the most powerful pieces of advice in the entire Quran.

"يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟" - "O you who believe!" Not "O you who disbelieve" but "O you who believe!" This is critical to understand.

"قُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ نَارًا" - "Protect yourselves and your families from a Fire." Think about this logically: Allah ﷻ wouldn't command us to protect ourselves from something we're not in danger of. If there's no threat, there's no need for protection. You would expect this command to be directed at disbelievers: "O you who disbelieve, protect yourselves from fire because you're heading toward it." But instead, it's addressed to believers.

This teaches us a profound and humbling lesson: just because we believe doesn't mean we or our families are safe from the Fire. We're not automatically protected. We have to actively engage in saving ourselves and our families from Hellfire.

Notice the order: "أَنفُسَكُمْ" comes before "وَأَهْلِيكُمْ" - yourselves before your families. If you're not actively trying to save yourself, you cannot save your family. This is a principle we see in action every day.

Consider the parents who bring their children to Sunday school and drop them off, then wait in the parking lot. The children pray inside while the parents shop or relax. It becomes cheap babysitting with a side benefit: maybe the kids will become a little more Muslim. Just Sunday school Muslim though - not too much, because if the teacher teaches too much Islam, they'll make sure to wear it off during the next six days.

I knew a Sunday school teacher who taught children the importance of Fajr prayer. The next week, a mother came fuming because her 13-year-old son started waking up for Fajr. She said he needs his sleep for basketball practice and told the teacher not to make the children so extreme.

The point is clear: you have to be concerned for yourself first. If you're not concerned for your own relationship with Allah ﷻ, what can you possibly give your children? You can't give them something you don't have.

I've had families ask me to speak to their children while the parents themselves were in spiritual crisis. I've given lectures where afterward, an elder would say "the youth really liked it" and walk away. But what about you? Why were you checking if others were sleeping instead of examining your own heart? Save yourself first.

When you genuinely work on yourself, your children will see it. Your family will see it. It won't be artificial. Then you'll actually be able to do something meaningful for your family.

What does it mean to save ourselves and our families from fire? It means being able to discuss our religion openly and naturally. Conversations about life, death, standing before Allah ﷻ, Jannah and Jahannam shouldn't be reserved for lectures or CDs played in the car. These should be normal dinner conversations, as natural as discussing sports.

We need to be real with our children about things that can lead them to Hellfire. We need these conversations consistently, not just when we see behavior we don't like. Too often, religion becomes an afterthought used only when we're embarrassed. A daughter starts dating, and suddenly we ask "isn't that haram?" - not because it violates Islamic principles, but because it's culturally embarrassing.

Yet all the inappropriate content we expose our children to at home through movies and shows - that's somehow acceptable? We maintain a facade of Muslim homes while consuming the same filth as everyone else. How can a family sit together watching content that would shame any person with dignity, with scenes of indecency and immorality, and then expect their children to have Islamic values?

We give young children smartphones with unrestricted internet access. Third graders have devices with apps that can download and hide content, password-protected so parents can't see, cloud-backed so even if deleted they can be restored. How is this protecting your children from fire?

For most people, life is simple: wake up, work, come home tired, eat, maybe watch a movie, sleep. Repeat. But our 24 hours need to be driven by the recognition that every day we're moving toward a goal. If our days aren't structured around learning and teaching our families, if we're not agenda-driven about their Islamic education, then it's just empty talk.

And yes, teaching them Quran and Arabic is important, but it must be accompanied by teaching proper character and humility. Otherwise we risk creating arrogance. Sometimes our children will have easy personalities, and sometimes they'll be difficult. That's part of the patience required in parenting. We have to persevere.

This ayah makes tawbah and family inseparable: "قُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ" - you can't just save yourself. You must save your family too. I once met a mother who told me she had two sons. One was becoming a hafiz, very righteous, praying tahajjud. The other had completely left Islam - going to clubs, doing drugs and alcohol, coming home every few weeks just to ask for money. She said, "I don't even consider him my son anymore. Only the righteous one is my son."

I told her: "Sister, I don't care what you say. He's your son." She brought up the example of Nuh (AS) and his son. But even when Allah ﷻ told Nuh (AS) to build the ark and board it, what did Nuh say? "My family!" Allah ﷻ had to explicitly reveal that the boy was not from his family. Until Allah ﷻ tells you explicitly, you cannot give up on your child.

You're his mother. You're obligated to at least try to counsel him, to connect him with good people, to have your other son reach out to him. So many families in America have daughters who ran away, who were secretly dating someone and the parents had no idea until it was too late. The response: "She's not my daughter anymore."

Yes, she is. She has a child with a non-Muslim partner? She's living with her boyfriend? It's horrible, yes. But it's done - now deal with it. She's still your daughter. She still needs guidance. She's not in Hell yet. She hasn't reached the point of no return. Nobody is at a point of no return while they're still alive.

You don't give up on family. When family members are far from the religion, you don't just call them to lecture them about Hellfire. You rebuild the relationship first. You visit them. You tolerate what's difficult. I know it will pain you to visit your cousin's house, your niece's house, your daughter's house and see her living with a non-Muslim partner with children born outside marriage. It will burn your heart. But you're not there for yourself. You're there because Allah ﷻ wants you to be there. You're there to re-establish the relationship, to soften hearts, asking Allah ﷻ to guide them, so that eventually they might return to Islam.

I knew a family where a girl had run away, left Islam, became Christian, married a Christian man, and had a child. Years later, because one sibling stayed in touch and didn't give up, she attended a program at the masjid. She brought her husband. They both became Muslim. She took her shahada - all because somebody in the family didn't give up on them.

This is what it means to protect your family from the Fire whose fuel is people and stones, guarded by stern angels who never disobey Allah ﷻ in what He commands them. This is the fire we're being warned about. This is what we must save ourselves and our families from, with Allah's ﷻ help and guidance.

translation: O you who have disbelieved, make no excuses that Day. You will only be recompensed for what you used to do
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ۖ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

This ayah presents something remarkable in the Quran's structure. It begins with يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (O you who disbelieve)—the only place in the entire Quran where disbelievers are directly addressed in this manner. What makes this even more striking is that it's sandwiched between two ayahs that both begin with يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (O you who believe).

This isn't simply a transition between different groups of people. It's showing us something profound about consequences. The believers in the previous ayah are warned to save their families from Hellfire, whose fuel is people and stones, whose angels are relentless. Now imagine someone listening to these descriptions—what are they picturing? They're envisioning the worst: someone in Hellfire.

That person who was addressed as a believer in the dunya but failed to save himself from Hellfire—once he's in that state, he won't be addressed as a believer anymore. How will he be addressed? As a disbeliever. This ayah is actually a scene from Hellfire itself, as the torture is happening, and in that moment it's being said: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ—O you who disbelieved, don't make excuses today.

Why are they told not to make excuses? Because the previous ayah already established the warning. You were told to believe in the danger of the Fire. You were told to protect yourself and your family. You were instructed to become proactive and take practical, serious measures. If you didn't act on that warning, you effectively denied it. At least in that warning, you behaved as a disbeliever. So don't make excuses now. Don't act like you didn't know. Ayah 6 is proof enough that you were told.

إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ—you're only being compensated for the very thing you were doing. It's not anything different. It's simply the consequence of your own behavior.

The believer who listens to these ayahs with genuine attention is shaken. Allah ﷻ in the dunya is calling me with يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟, but if I don't get my act together, I will be addressed in the Akhirah as يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟. This thought should rattle our minds and shake our hearts. That realization leads directly into the next ayah.

translation: O you who have believed, repent to Allah with sincere repentance. Perhaps your Lord will remove from you your misdeeds and admit you into gardens beneath which rivers flow [on] the Day when Allah will not disgrace the Prophet and those who believed with him. Their light will proceed before them and on their right; they will say, "Our Lord, perfect for us our light and forgive us. Indeed, You are over all things competent
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوْبَةً نَّصُوحًا عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُكَفِّرَ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَيُدْخِلَكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ ۖ نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَىٰ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

If the previous ayahs shook you up, then get your act together. يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوْبَةً نَّصُوحًا—O you who believe, make tawbah to Allah, a sincere, immediate, one-time, complete tawbah.

Understanding the word تَوْبَةً نَّصُوحًا requires exploring the different forms of the word tawbah in Arabic. There are three primary forms: تَوْبَة, تَوْب, and مَتَاب. Each appears in the Quran in specific contexts. The strongest form, matāb, appears in Surah Al-Furqan where Allah ﷻ discusses taking someone who has made sincere tawbah and converting all their evil deeds into good deeds—that's an extraordinary level of repentance deserving the strongest term.

Generally, Allah ﷻ accepts all kinds of tawbah, and the general word for that is simply tawb, as when He says قَابِلِ ٱلتَّوْبِ (Accepter of repentance). But here, Allah ﷻ uses تَوْبَةً with a specific qualification: نَصُوحًا.

The form tawbatan is what's called ism al-marrah in Arabic—it indicates a single, decisive instance. Allah ﷻ is calling for one solid, sincere, final, life-changing tawbah. Not repentance followed by returning to sin a day later, then saying "Astaghfirullah" again in a cycle. Make a decision. Make one tawbah and do it right. Make that final decision.

Consider when someone takes shahadah—their life changes forever. They've made that one monumental tawbah to Allah ﷻ. Shahadah itself is a form of tawbah: repenting from all previous lifestyle choices and returning to Allah ﷻ. That's the kind of tawbah Allah ﷻ wants believers to have when they hear this ayah. You need to make a serious decision.

The word نَصُوحًا itself includes the meaning of sincerity and ikhlas—you mean what you're saying. 

عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ—perhaps your Master. Allah ﷻ uses "perhaps" because you haven't made the tawbah yet. This isn't expressing doubt about Allah's acceptance; rather, the condition depends on your action. The ball is in your court. If you make sincere tawbah, it will be accepted. If you don't, or if your tawbah isn't genuine, complete or sincere—if it's merely seasonal, like a phase that passes—then the outcome remains uncertain.

Think about Hajj. It's supposed to be a time of nasūḥ—sincere repentance. You're in that powerful environment, seeing the Kaaba, surrounded by people crying more than taking selfies. Tears flow down your face and you make tawbah. But then a week later, two weeks later, a year later, you're no longer in that scene. You're somewhere else—walking through a mall, passing a store with inappropriate content. Where did that commitment go?

Allah ﷻ is calling for something deeper: make a serious decision to change the entire course and direction of life for yourself and your families. This requires genuinely serious thought and planning. This is the kind of conversation husband and wife should have together. That love they have for each other should be so strong that they don't want to see each other in pain in the Akhirah, or their children suffering. They sit together and make plans for keeping their children spiritually safe.

If you install a security system in your house, lock the doors every time, don't let the kids near the pool, keep knives away from young children, hide tools that a baby might swallow—you have this natural urge to protect your children in the dunya. A believer says: I want to protect my children both in the dunya and in the Akhirah. Just as we take precautions here, we must take spiritual precautions as well.

عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُكَفِّرَ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَيُدْخِلَكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ—perhaps your Master will remove your evil deeds and enter you into gardens beneath which rivers flow.

يَوْمَ لَا يُخْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ—the Day on which Allah ﷻ will not humiliate His Prophet ﷺ and those who believed with him. The word مَعَهُۥ (ma'ahu, with him) carries significant weight. It's not just وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (those who believed), but specifically وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ (those who believed with him). This is an important qualification.

Many people claim to believe, but those who believe and then remain steadfast with the Prophet ﷺ—loyal to his cause, his mission, his instructions—are distinguished from others. This ma'ahu creates a distinction. We've already learned in this surah that there are some with him and some not with him, with a wall separating them. That's why this ma'ahu matters so profoundly here.

نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَىٰ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ—their light will extend from in front of them and on their right. This imagery was also mentioned in Surah Al-Hadid, where a comparison was made between hypocrites and believers. Here in At-Tahrim, something additional is highlighted. Those on the good side of the wall, those who are ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ—with the Prophet ﷺ—are described in greater detail regarding what happens on the right side of that wall.

As these believers proceed, even though they've successfully saved themselves, they haven't reached Jannah yet. So now they turn to Allah ﷻ with a powerful supplication.

The Prophet ﷺ said that some of the Sahaba (RA) and believers will have light so strong it will illuminate the distance from Madinah to San'a in Yemen. Imagine stadium lights that cost millions of dollars and can be seen for ten or twelve miles—now imagine that kind of light emanating from a single believer, traveling from one city to another city in a different country. And there are others whose light barely illuminates their own feet, forcing them to watch every single step, uncertain of what they're stepping on or into.

نُورُهُمْ يَسْعَىٰ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَبِأَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ أَتْمِمْ لَنَا نُورَنَا—their light extending before them and on their right, they're saying, "Our Master, complete our light for us."

This supplication reveals profound spiritual awareness. Just because you can see the destination doesn't mean you'll make it there. As you're walking with this light, you're filled with concern, asking Allah ﷻ to maintain it until the very end. وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَآ—and forgive us. They understand that the only thing that can cause the light to disappear is their mistakes. So they plead: just keep covering our errors. We know we have plenty of them. Just cover them and maintain our light.

إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ—You are certainly in complete control over all things. This acknowledgment of Allah's absolute power frames their supplication with proper understanding of His capability.

translation: O Prophet, strive against the disbelievers and the hypocrites and be harsh upon them. And their refuge is Hell, and wretched is the destination
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ جَـٰهِدِ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

Now consider: in Ayah 7, people who didn't try to save themselves—believers who neglected to protect themselves—were addressed as ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (those who disbelieved). Though we cannot call such people disbelievers in this world (in the dunya, the proper term would be منافقين, hypocrites), in the Akhirah they're labeled as disbelievers. In a spiritual sense, as far as Allah ﷻ is concerned, they become one and the same category.

What does He say in this ayah? يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ جَـٰهِدِ ٱلْكُفَّارَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ—O Prophet, struggle against the disbelievers and the hypocrites. Struggling against open disbelievers is straightforward enough. But the real problem highlighted in this surah isn't those fighting on the battlefield. The problem is those who don't care about saving themselves, who don't care about saving their families, who have other priorities on their minds. To this entire mission, they are a liability.

وَٱغْلُظْ عَلَيْهِمْ—and be harsh with them, take a firm stance against them. وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ—and their final refuge, the place they take shelter, is Jahannam. وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ—and what a horrible place to return to.

translation: Allah presents an example of those who disbelieved: the wife of Noah and the wife of Lot. They were under two of Our righteous servants but betrayed them, so those prophets did not avail them from Allah at all, and it was said, "Enter the Fire with those who enter
commentary:
ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱمْرَأَتَ نُوحٍ وَٱمْرَأَتَ لُوطٍ ۖ كَانَتَا تَحْتَ عَبْدَيْنِ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا صَـٰلِحَيْنِ فَخَانَتَاهُمَا فَلَمْ يُغْنِيَا عَنْهُمَا مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا وَقِيلَ ٱدْخُلَا ٱلنَّارَ مَعَ ٱلدَّٰخِلِينَ

Allah ﷻ sets forth an example for the disbelievers: the wives of Nuh (AS) and Lut (AS). These two women were under two of Our righteous servants, yet they betrayed them. The word used here is فَخَانَتَاهُمَا– they were dishonest to them. The root خ ا ن carries the meaning of dishonesty and betrayal, a profound breach of trust.

What's remarkable is that despite being married to prophets of Allah ﷻ, their status provided them no benefit whatsoever. فَلَمْ يُغْنِيَا عَنْهُمَا مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا – "Then they did not benefit them against Allah at all." These women perhaps thought their position as wives of prophets gave them special privileges, similar to how being married to someone in power in this world brings certain advantages. But those connections meant nothing before Allah ﷻ.

The Prophet ﷺ himself told his own family, "Save yourselves from the Fire. I cannot help you against Allah." This is the same message for everyone – no relationship, no lineage, no connection will save anyone from Allah's judgment if they choose disbelief and betrayal.

The ayah concludes with their fate: وَقِيلَ ٱدْخُلَا ٱلنَّارَ مَعَ ٱلدَّٰخِلِينَ – "And it was said, 'Enter the Fire with those who enter.'" Notice the specificity here. Earlier in the surah, when the believers were mentioned, they were described as being "with the Prophet ﷺ." Now, these two women are commanded to enter the Fire "with those who enter" – grouped with the general inhabitants of Hell, not given any special distinction despite their former positions.

translation: And Allah presents an example of those who believed: the wife of Pharaoh, when she said, "My Lord, build for me near You a house in Paradise and save me from Pharaoh and his deeds and save me from the wrongdoing people
commentary:
وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱمْرَأَتَ فِرْعَوْنَ إِذْ قَالَتْ رَبِّ ٱبْنِ لِى عِندَكَ بَيْتًا فِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَنَجِّنِى مِن فِرْعَوْنَ وَعَمَلِهِۦ وَنَجِّنِى مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Allah ﷻ now gives another example – this time for those who believe, as an encouragement: the wife of Fir'awn. Consider her circumstances. She lived in one of the most stable and magnificent structures ever built, so enduring that it remains a tourist attraction millennia later. She was a queen in one of the greatest empires in human history. The Egyptian civilization was so advanced that other kings sent their children there for education. The Greeks and Romans built their columns and architecture inspired by Egyptian design. Even the American dollar bill bears the pyramid, a recognition by the founding fathers of that civilization's greatness.

This woman had everything the world could offer. If anyone had the opportunity to live the life of luxury and do whatever they pleased, it was her. She was wrapped in more دنيا (worldly comfort) than perhaps any woman in history – luxury, power, protection, prestige. Yet her heart found no contentment in any of it.

She lived in what was probably one of the greatest homes architecturally ever constructed on this planet, and yet she made this dua: رَبِّ ٱبْنِ لِى عِندَكَ بَيْتًا فِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ – "My Lord, build for me a house with You in Paradise." She wanted a house near Allah ﷻ, the true Rabb. She lived with someone who claimed to be رب (Fir'awn declared himself lord), but she knew better. She wanted to be near the Real Rabb.

Her dua continues: وَنَجِّنِى مِن فِرْعَوْنَ وَعَمَلِهِۦ – "And rescue me from Fir'awn and his work." Then she adds: وَنَجِّنِى مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ – "And rescue me from the wrongdoing people."

What's beautiful about this dua is its connection to another part of the Quran. This same woman took in baby Musa (AS) and played a role in raising him. She taught him something profound – how to make dua to Allah ﷻ, how to get close to Him. Before revelation, before the mountain, before the Book, when Musa (AS) accidentally killed a man and had to flee Egypt, he said: رَبِّ نَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ – "My Lord, save me from the wrongdoing people."

Where did Musa (AS) learn this dua? From his stepmother, the wife of Fir'awn. There are no accidents in the Quran. She saw this boy being raised in the palace, and while he could have lived the comfortable life too, she gave him something far greater – she taught him to turn to Allah ﷻ. The command in this surah is قُوا أَنفُسَكُمْ وَأَهْلِيكُمْ نَارًا – "Save yourselves and your families from the Fire." She saw him as family and tried to save him by teaching him this connection to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And [the example of] Mary, the daughter of 'Imran, who guarded her chastity, so We blew into [her garment] through Our angel, and she believed in the words of her Lord and His scriptures and was of the devoutly obedient
commentary:
وَمَرْيَمَ ٱبْنَتَ عِمْرَٰنَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِنَا وَصَدَّقَتْ بِكَلِمَـٰتِ رَبِّهَا وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَكَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ

Finally, Allah ﷻ presents Maryam (AS), the daughter of Imran. The ayah describes her as ٱلَّتِىٓ أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا – "the one who guarded her chastity." She was extraordinarily cautious about protecting her modesty and honor.

Then Allah ﷻ says: فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِنَا – "So We blew into it from Our spirit," referring to the miraculous conception of Isa (AS). This was going to be traumatic for Maryam (AS) – an incredibly difficult trial. But she accepted it.

The ayah continues: وَصَدَّقَتْ بِكَلِمَـٰتِ رَبِّهَا – "And she believed in the words of her Lord." When the angel came to her with the news, even though she was frightened and said, "I seek refuge in the Most Merciful from you if you are God-fearing," she ultimately confirmed the truth of what Allah ﷻ told her would happen.

Notice the next phrase: وَكُتُبِهِۦ – "and His Books" (plural). Maryam (AS) wasn't just a righteous woman; she was a scholar. She had mastered the Torah and the Injeel. She confirmed the truth in all of them. If it were just one book, the word would be singular, but Allah ﷻ uses the plural, showing us that she even learned and mastered the book her son was given.

The ayah concludes: وَكَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ – "And she was of the devoutly obedient." The word قانتين refers to those who are subservient, obedient, and loyal to Allah ﷻ. What's remarkable here is that Maryam (AS) was not even married, yet Allah ﷻ describes her with this quality of قنوت. Usually, when obedience and devotion are mentioned in the context of women, people immediately think of obedience to a husband. But Allah ﷻ is teaching us something profound: the first and primary subservience is to Allah ﷻ Himself. Through this, He's instructing the mothers of the believers, whose situation opened this surah, that their قنوت to the Prophet ﷺ is directly connected to their قنوت to Allah ﷻ.

### The Pattern of Examples

These final ayahs present a remarkable pattern. The surah mentions three categories of women, each representing different combinations of environment and character:

First, there's a great husband and family environment with a terrible wife – the cases of Nuh's (AS) and Lut's (AS) wives. The righteous environment couldn't help them because of their choices.

Second, there's the opposite: an awesome woman in a terrible environment – the wife of Fir'awn. The corrupt environment couldn't corrupt her because of her faith and connection to Allah ﷻ.

Third, there's a great environment with a great woman – Maryam (AS). She was under the care of Zakariya (AS), in the house of Allah ﷻ, guarding her chastity, learning the books of Allah ﷻ. Both environment and character came together in excellence.

Actually, there are two examples of great women in great environments. The surah begins with the mothers of the believers and ends with Maryam (AS) – both examples of righteous women in blessed circumstances. What's beautiful is the contrast: one environment (the mothers of the believers) is a product of marriage, while the other (Maryam) shows that even without marriage, a woman can achieve the highest levels of excellence. Both situations merit the mention of قنوت.

There's one scenario deliberately missing from this surah: negative environment with negative character – a horrible husband with a horrible wife. That situation is so terrible that it gets its own surah: Surah Al-Masad, about Abu Lahab and his wife. They deserved each other, so they're mentioned separately. Their mention doesn't infect this beautiful surah.

All these scenarios are templates for the kinds of situations that will exist in this world. Throughout the surah, from the opening passage about the Prophet's ﷺ household, to the discussion of potential replacement wives and their qualities, to these final examples, one characteristic keeps appearing: قنوت – devoted obedience and subservience.

### Understanding Qunoot in Marriage

The quality of قنوت deserves careful reflection, especially in our contemporary context. Allah ﷻ set a certain organizational structure in the household from the very beginning. The first human being sent to earth was not sent alone – Adam (AS) was sent with his wife. Even in Jannah, he wasn't alone.

When Allah ﷻ addresses them, He says: يَا آدَمُ اسْكُنْ أَنتَ وَزَوْجُكَ الْجَنَّةَ – "O Adam, dwell you and your wife in Paradise." The command to settle down was given to Adam (AS), but it included his wife. Allah ﷻ could have said اسْكُنَا (both of you settle), but instead He addresses the head of the household, and that address extends to the entire household.

This same pattern appears at the beginning of this surah. The entire community needs to be addressed, but it's sufficient to address the father of that community – the Prophet ﷺ. The surah opens: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذَا طَلَّقْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ – "O Prophet, when you divorce women..." The address to him as the head extends to the entire believing community.

This is the structure Allah ﷻ established in the family unit. Now, this is a general principle, and there are exceptions – the Quran mentions extremes, like the wife of Fir'awn begging for escape. Every variation exists: abusive husbands, abusive wives, good husbands, good wives, and everything in between. But we don't abandon the general principle just because our specific situation feels different.

The husband is an أمير in the household. This doesn't mean absolute boss, but it does mean leader – the one who holds the final decision. A household cannot function if he's not allowed to make that final decision, and it cannot function if the wife doesn't trust his decisions or undermines them. When parents contradict each other, children learn to disrespect both and manipulate the division between them.

But what does being an أمير actually mean? The Prophet ﷺ said: سَيِّدُ الْقَوْمِ خَادِمُهُمْ – "The leader of a people is their servant." When a man is a real أمير, he takes care of his wife and children so well, with such love, courtesy and forgiveness, that he commands respect without demanding it. They would never want to disappoint him – not because they fear his anger, but because of how honorable he is.

Being an أمير is not a license to get angry. That's the last resort, and it's rarely sanctioned anyway. The Prophet ﷺ would get angry on rare occasions, and his anger manifested as silence. If someone wants to follow the Sunnah, they should know that.

When a husband fulfills his role properly, being patient and forgiving, this loosens the reins a bit. And when things are loose, there's a possibility they might be taken advantage of. This is what happened in the situation that opened this surah. Allah ﷻ revealed ayat making it clear that the Prophet's ﷺ kindness should not be exploited: فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ مَوْلَاهُ وَجِبْرِيلُ وَصَالِحُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ – Allah ﷻ would not let that happen in the Prophet's ﷺ case.

In any marriage, when the husband is soft and courteous – as he's commanded to be – there's a possibility the wife might take advantage. Just because he doesn't say anything doesn't mean there's open license to do whatever one wants. Just because he doesn't yell and scream doesn't mean boundaries don't exist. There must be respect for his requests and sensitivity to his needs.

On the husband's side, there needs to be real education about what it means to lead a family. On the wife's side, there needs to be understanding of what it means to be supportive and devoted. Both sides come to understand their roles through healthy شورى (mutual consultation).

### Practical Wisdom for Marriage

No human being on earth likes being told what to do. Men don't like it. Women don't like it. It's in our nature. So when a wife doesn't immediately want to follow an instruction, that's natural. It takes work. Husbands need to be sensitive to this reality.

Give as little instruction as possible. Only instruct when absolutely necessary. The Prophet ﷺ washed his own clothes and did his own dishes. Be as independent as you can be. Expect as little as possible from your wife, because when you expect very little, even small gestures become appreciated as miracles from Allah ﷻ. When you expect a lot, you'll spend your life upset about an endless list of things that didn't get done.

If a husband learns to be independent and still takes care of his wife's needs, then the wife should recognize this and think, "He's doing so much on his own and for the household. I should help him more. He's already doing so much; I should contribute too." This shouldn't come from religious obligation alone, but from genuine desire to support each other.

Don't rub things in when you do something for your spouse. If you iron your husband's shirt, don't mention it later. If you're going to bring it up, don't do it at all. If you make breakfast, don't remind him that you made it. Seriously, he's better off without breakfast than hearing about it later. The same goes for husbands – if you do something for your wife, don't bring it up. Just do it and leave it.

If we keep Allah ﷻ at the heart of our marriage, recognizing that the إحسان (excellence) we show each other is an act of worship to Allah ﷻ, then bringing things up becomes unthinkable. The One who needs to appreciate our efforts has already appreciated them – and that's not our spouse, it's Allah ﷻ. He appreciates the gesture you made to another servant of His.

Keep this perspective, and you'll have a happy marriage. Without it, you'll have a miserable one.

May Allah ﷻ grant us all happy marriages and help us remind each other to better ourselves. May He make our husbands the best of husbands, our wives the best of wives, our parents the best of parents, and our children the best of children.
